cataloging_subject.label: Promptorium parvulorum
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous bilingual Middle English-Latin lexicon associated with a Dominican friar of King's Lynn, Norfolk, and dated 1440 in the Harley redaction, including its Latin preamble and alphabetic word-list. The record does not identify this abstract medieval work with Harley MS 221 or any other physical witness, the early printed versions, Albert Way's three-part 1843-1865 scholarly edition and commentary, A. L. Mayhew's 1908 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Promptorium parvulorum
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Dominican compiler of King's Lynn; Richard Fraunces tentatively proposed; older Geoffrey the Grammarian attribution disputed
catalog_summary.date: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction; first printed 15 May 1499
catalog_summary.region: King's Lynn and Norfolk, England; the compiler explicitly followed Norfolk usage, while the Harley base witness is probably not East Anglian

assertion.notes: The title is stated at the end of the encoded preamble and is the established short title in the current British Library record.
Way records this form from King's College MS 8.
The 1499 Pynson prologue uses this form.
Way records this title from Wynkyn de Worde's editions.

scope.description: Title applied to the Promptorium in the 1499 colophon and later printed editions

assertion.notes: This historical printed title must not be confused with the distinct Latin-English work also called Medulla grammaticae.

titles.value: Promptorium Parvulorum sive Clericorum, Dictionarius Anglo-Latinus Princeps

scope.description: Shortened form of the title of Albert Way's immediate source edition

agents.name: Anonymous Dominican compiler of King's Lynn
notes: The preamble identifies one Dominican friar or preacher bound to poverty at Lynn and the explicit dates the compilation to 1440, but no surviving manuscript names him.

agents.name: Geoffrey the Grammarian
notes: Traditional attribution based on Bale and a later annotation in a Cambridge copy of Pynson's 1499 edition; the current British Library catalog retains a question mark, while John Considine's 2022 reconstruction rejects it as a post-medieval bibliographical mistake.

agents.name: Richard Fraunces
notes: Tentatively proposed by John Considine from an annotation reported by Thomas Hearne in a copy of Pynson's 1499 edition. The annotation's description of the compiler as an enclosed anchorite is independent of the printed preamble and agrees with Harley MS 221, but the annotated copy is not currently available for direct inspection.

agents.name: Albert Way
notes: 1805-1874; editor and collator of the three-part Camden Society edition issued in 1843, 1853, and 1865.

assertion.notes: The work self-identifies its compiler as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn, but does not supply a personal name.

This records the historical attribution rather than endorsing it. No medieval Promptorium witness names Geoffrey; Considine reconstructs the attribution as a mistake arising from Bale's use of printed grammatical Sammelbande, and the distinct Medulla grammaticae has also been confused with this work.

Considine treats Richard Fraunces as a plausible identification, not a certainty: Hearne's reported annotation supplies the name and the independently corroborated anchorite detail, but no extant medieval witness names its compiler.

scope.description: Immediate 1843-1865 source edition and its commentary
Harley MS 221 base text with readings and additions from five other manuscripts and three early printed editions

assertion.notes: The explicit following the preamble dates the collection of the vocabulary to Anno Domini 1440. Considine cautions that this may date the expanded Harley redaction or may preserve knowledge of the original compilation date; it is therefore retained as the conventional work date with qualified confidence.
The compiler identifies himself as a Dominican recluse of Lynn; this supports the compilation association without proving a specific building or anchorage.
The preamble says the compiler followed the Norfolk manner of speaking learned from infancy. The British Library separately cautions that Harley MS 221 itself is probably not East Anglian.

scope.description: English headwords, synonyms, and explanatory phrases

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin preamble, equivalents, grammatical labels, citations, and source list

notes: The work is genuinely bilingual: Latin is structural content, not merely occasional quotation.

form_statements.description: A prose preamble followed by alphabetically arranged lexical entries; no intrinsic verse section is modeled.

assertion.notes: The preamble says the work was compiled for young clerics seeking Latin vocabulary.

summaries.value: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction and designed for learners who needed ready access to Latin vocabulary, the Promptorium parvulorum pairs Middle English headwords and explanatory phrases with Latin equivalents and grammatical information. Its compiler identifies himself only as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn who followed Norfolk speech. The conventional name Geoffrey the Grammarian is a later, disputed attribution; Richard Fraunces is a plausible but unverified alternative. The medieval arrangement separates nomina from verba within alphabetic sections; Way's immediate source edition instead merges them into one alphabetic sequence and adds extensive commentary.

The repository XML has two 1843/1853 volume-title divisions, the medieval preamble, 23 letter divisions containing 11,797 direct lexicon ITEM nodes, Way's local notes and early-print colophons through printed p. 540, and four pages of additional notes/corrections. It omits the 1865 title, Advertisement, Way's long Preface and Appendix, the orthographic index on pp. 541-550, the subject index on pp. 551-560, and binder matter. These are encoding metrics and selection facts, not a canonical medieval headword count.

extent.description: Encoding metrics: one preamble plus 23 direct alphabetic DIV3 sections; 11,797 direct DIV3/LIST/ITEM lexicon entries. The XML also contains 1,949 notes, three verse groups and 825 L nodes inside Way's editorial quotations and apparatus; those lines are not intrinsic verse structure of the medieval lexicon.

content_parts.label: Preambulum
content_parts.summary: Latin explanation of the work's educational purpose, sources, alphabetic organization, Norfolk usage, correction request, compiler status, and 1440 date.

content_parts.label: Alphabetic lexicon
content_parts.summary: Middle English headwords and explanatory phrases with Latin equivalents, serialized by Way and the XML in 23 letter divisions from A through yogh.
notes: The 11,797 count includes direct encoded ITEM entries under the letter divisions and excludes nested editorial lists and the 37 additional-notes ITEM nodes.
The three LG and 825 L nodes occur in Way's quoted illustrative material and do not make the medieval lexicon mixed-form.

related_works.title: Medulla grammaticae
notes: A distinct Latin-English dictionary repeatedly confused with the Promptorium because early Promptorium colophons use Medulla grammatice as an alternate title and later authorities attributed both works to Geoffrey.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML for CME00028 at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; title divisions; preamble; lexicon; early-print notices; additional notes and corrections
evidence.summary: Establishes exact repository identity, title forms, compiler self-description, 1440 date, Norfolk-language statement, bilingual content, Way edition text and apparatus, encoded selection, and final boundary.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XPath and repository inspection of CME00028.xml.
evidence.locator: count(//DIV1[@TYPE='lexicon']/DIV2/DIV3/LIST/ITEM)=11797; 23 DIV3 letter sections; 1949 NOTE; 37 addendum ITEM; 546 PB; 3 LG; 825 L
evidence.summary: Establishes encoding metrics and distinguishes direct lexicon entries from nested editorial material and addenda.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00028 item information and table of contents.
evidence.locator: item information; contents; rights and permissions
evidence.summary: Confirms stable identifier, inherited long title and author heading, 1843-65 publication statement, public text access, subject headings, letter divisions, notes, and U-M's current rights assertion.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: States that the 2016-2018 batch was manually keyed from public-domain editions, proofread and edited in house, and marked up with basic TEI semantics; CME00028's TEMPHEAD review is dated 2016-07-15.

evidence.citation: Albert Way, ed., Promptorium Parvulorum sive Clericorum, Camden Society nos. 25, 54, and 89 (London, 1843, 1853, 1865), complete bound three-part scan.
evidence.locator: title pages; Advertisement pp. v-xi; Preface pp. xiii-xlix; medieval preamble pp. 1-4; lexicon pp. 5-540; indexes pp. 541-560; additional notes pp. [560]-563; binder instruction
evidence.summary: Direct inspection establishes Way's title, three-part issue dates, Harley MS 221 base text, five manuscript collations, three early printed comparison texts, editorial rearrangement and normalization, complete printed scope, and the matter omitted from CME00028.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 221.
evidence.locator: scope and content; folio contents; physical characteristics; origin; publications
evidence.summary: Current exact record for Way's base witness: parchment, fifteenth century, ff. 206 plus preliminary and end leaves; Promptorium at ff. 1r-205v and adjacent recipes at f. 206r. It describes the 1440 compilation, later Geoffrey attribution, sources, organization, known witnesses, and cautions that the manuscript itself is probably not East Anglian.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2274 and component ff. 63r-77v.
evidence.locator: component scope; contents; parent physical characteristics
evidence.summary: Confirms a disordered Promptorium fragment on ff. 63r-77v, containing G, H, M, N, O, and R, within a 77-folio composite paper/parchment miscellany dated to the second half of the fifteenth century.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 22556.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark and imperfect fifteenth-century parchment witness, with modern Way letter and notes at ff. 1-3 and surviving Promptorium material at ff. 5r-110v.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 37789.
evidence.locator: Promptorium scope and ff. 1r-83v; Medulla ff. 84r-174v; physical characteristics; provenance
evidence.summary: Crosswalks Way's H and former Phillipps MS 8306/Heber 1360 to current Add MS 37789, a late-fifteenth-century Norfolk paper codex containing an imperfect Promptorium followed by the distinct Medulla grammaticae.

evidence.citation: A. L. Mayhew, ed., The Promptorium Parvulorum: The First English-Latin Dictionary, EETS Extra Series 102 (London, 1908).
evidence.locator: title page; Introduction pp. vii-xxviii; manuscript census pp. xxvi-xxviii
evidence.summary: Later comparison edition from Winchester Cathedral MS 15. It describes Way's six manuscripts and sigla, the early editions, compiler and attribution history, Winchester witness, and Way's editorial transformation.

evidence.citation: Penn Libraries Colenda, Inc P-1011 Kislak copy, Promptorius paruulorum siue clericorum (Richard Pynson, 1499).
evidence.locator: catalog description; leaves a1v-t3v; colophon; physical copy; IIIF canvases 8-232
evidence.summary: Current institutional record and complete facsimile establish the 15 May 1499 imprint, alternate titles, Latin prologue, Middle English-Latin word-list, colophon, signatures, 116-leaf ideal extent, and later author attribution caveat.

evidence.citation: Gesamtkatalog der Wiegendrucke, GW 10483, Promptorium parvulorum.
evidence.locator: imprint, collation, incipit, colophon, census and reproductions
evidence.summary: Independent incunable authority confirms Richard Pynson's London printing for Frederick Egmondt and Petrus post Pascha on 15 May 1499, the 116-leaf collation, and surviving copies.

evidence.citation: John Considine, Sixteenth-Century English Dictionaries (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2022), chapter 1, pp. 20-25.
evidence.locator: chapter 1, section 'The Catholicon Anglicum and the Promptorium parvulorum', especially pp. 20-25
evidence.summary: Recent scholarship reconstructs the Promptorium as an accreting branch of the Medulla tradition, distinguishes the original work from the expanded Harley and Winchester redactions used by the modern editors, identifies nine extant whole or fragmentary fifteenth-century witnesses, rejects the Geoffrey attribution as Bale-derived, and argues that Hearne's reported Richard Fraunces attribution is plausible but not certain.

evidence.locator: access for researchers; manuscript catalog information
evidence.summary: Current institutional access route for rare books and manuscripts; the exact MS 8 identification and historical description are supplied by Way, Mayhew, and the current British Library witness census.

evidence.locator: manuscript collection and research enquiry information
evidence.summary: Current institutional page says manuscript records remain chiefly printed or handwritten and provides a curator enquiry route; it exposes no item-level digital record or whole-object facsimile for MS 15.

open_questions.question: Can the compiler's personal identity be established by directly verifying the lost-or-unlocated annotation naming Richard Fraunces or through independent Dominican records?
next_steps: Trace the 1499 copy formerly owned by Thomas Ward of Longbridge in which Thomas Hearne reported the Richard Fraunces annotation and inspect the inscription if the copy survives.

Compare the name and anchorite description with King's Lynn Dominican, episcopal, testamentary, and enclosure records; separately reassess the Bale-derived Geoffrey annotation in Cambridge University Library Inc.3.J.3.6[3619].

notes: The exact sources named after the medieval preamble include the Catholicon, Campus florum, Dictionarius and works associated with Huguccio, William Brito, John of Garland, Robert Kilwardby, Alexander Neckam and others; uncertain modern identifications are not converted into direct lineage entities.

Way's printed title-page claim that the work is by Frater Galfridus is preserved as edition-level attribution, not treated as a medieval self-identification; current scholarship instead treats Richard Fraunces as a plausible but unverified name.
The XML's TEXT/@LANG value 'enm lat' correctly signals a bilingual work but does not describe Way's surrounding modern English commentary as part of the abstract medieval work.
